candidate.question: Will 2026 rank as the sixth-hottest year on record or lower?

event.title: Where will 2026 rank among the hottest years on record?
event.description:
This market will resolve according to the numerical rank of how hot 2026 is when compared against all other years for which the Global Land-Ocean Temperature Index has data.

Years will be ranked in descending order, starting with the hottest as number 1, the second hottest as number 2, etc.

If 2026 ties with any other year, it will resolve according to the place the year it ties with occupies.

This market will resolve immediately once the specified data becomes available, regardless of whether the figure for the relevant years is later revised.

The primary resolution source for this market will be the figure found in the table titled "Land-Ocean Temperature Index (C)" under the column "No_Smoothing" in the row "2026" (https://data.giss.nasa.gov/gistemp/graphs/graph_data/Global_Mean_Estimates_based_on_Land_and_Ocean_Data/graph.txt). If NASA's "Global Temperature Index" is rendered permanently unavailable, other information from NASA may be used. If no information for 2026 is provide...

eventMetadata.context_description: Trader consensus on Polymarket heavily favors 2026 ranking as the second-hottest year on record (56.5% implied probability), with a strong 37% chance of claiming first, reflecting recent Copernicus ERA5 data showing the first four months averaging top-five warmest globally—January and February fifth, March fourth, and April joint-third behind only 2024 and 2025 peaks. NOAA's latest ENSO outlook (April 9) projects a 61% chance of El Niño emerging in May-July and persisting through year-end, fueled by subsurface Pacific warming and westerly winds, which could add 0.1–0.2°C to annual anomalies amid ongoing anthropogenic forcing near 1.5°C above pre-industrial levels. Uncertainties in El Niño strength persist; upcoming May temperature bulletins and weekly ENSO updates from NOAA and Copernicus will refine annual projections against 2024's record.

candidate.event_title: 2026 May 1st, 2nd, 3rd hottest on record?
candidate.question: Will May 2026 be the 3rd hottest on record?

event.title: 2026 May 1st, 2nd, 3rd hottest on record?
event.description:
This market will resolve based on the data for the Global Land-Ocean Temperature Index for May 2026 versus the data points available for all other Mays on record. 

Note: If May 2026 is tied for first, second, or third hottest with another year, it will qualify for the bracket it ties with.

The primary resolution source for this market will be the figures found in the table titled "GLOBAL Land-Ocean Temperature Index in 0.01 degrees Celsius" under the column "May" (https://data.giss.nasa.gov/gistemp/tabledata_v4/GLB.Ts+dSST.txt). If NASA's "Global Temperature Index" is rendered permanently unavailable, other information from NASA may be used.
If no information for May 2026 is provided by NASA by June 30, 2026, 11:59 PM ET, a consensus of credible sources will be used to resolve this market.

eventMetadata.context_description: Preliminary ERA5 reanalysis data from Copernicus through May 12, 2026, indicate global surface air temperature anomalies tracking at or above May 2024's record levels—the previous hottest May—elevating trader consensus to 53.5% implied probability for a new record, with 33.5% for second-hottest behind 2024 and 10.1% for third. This positioning stems from sustained above-average sea surface temperatures, neutral-to-El Niño ENSO transition forecasted at 61% likelihood for May-July by NOAA, and April 2026's joint third-warmest ranking, continuing post-2024 warming trends despite slight yearly cooldown. Historical analogs show the past three Mays dominating records amid accelerating climate-driven heat; remaining fortnight data and NOAA's mid-June report could shift outcomes amid inherent forecast uncertainty.
